title: The Framing of Decisions and the Psychology of Choice

evaluation_framework.description: Two-tier evaluation: phenomenon-level and data-level matching

type_1.name: phenomenon_level_match
type_1.description: Tests whether agent exhibits the target psychological phenomenon (framing effect)
type_1.approach: Uses original statistical methods from the paper (p-values, significance tests)

type_2.name: data_level_match
type_2.description: Tests how closely agent data matches human baseline data
type_2.approach: Uses effect size metrics (Cohen's h) to quantify similarity

positive_frame_results.note: 72% chose certain option (200 saved), 25% chose risky option. Percentages don't sum to 100 due to rounding or missing responses.

negative_frame_results.note: 22% chose certain option (400 die), 78% chose risky option. Demonstrates preference reversal.

framing_effect.description: Preference reversal between frames despite identical expected values
framing_effect.interpretation: In positive frame, 72% risk-averse (choose certain gain). In negative frame, 78% risk-seeking (choose risky loss). 50 percentage point difference demonstrates strong framing effect.

overall.framing_effect_note: 53 percentage point difference in certain option choice

main_effect.conclusion: Choice preferences significantly differ between positive and negative frames despite objectively identical options

required_tests.description: Tests whether agent exhibits framing effect (cognitive phenomenon)

method.rationale: Original paper used chi-square test, χ²(1) = 41.7, p < 0.001

required_tests.description: Tests whether effect direction matches human pattern (positive > negative)

method.rationale: Positive frame should show more risk aversion than negative frame

required_tests.description: Measures how closely agent data matches human data in positive frame

method.rationale: Cohen's h quantifies difference between two proportions (Cohen, 1988)

required_tests.description: Measures how closely agent data matches human data in negative frame
Measures how closely the magnitude of framing effect matches human data

score_ranges.0.50-0.69: Partial replication, framing effect present but weaker
score_ranges.0.00-0.49: Failed replication, no clear framing effect

key_findings.main_finding: Preference reversal induced by framing: 72% risk-averse in gain frame vs 78% risk-seeking in loss frame
key_findings.theoretical_implication: Demonstrates violation of invariance principle in rational choice theory
key_findings.practical_implication: How choices are framed significantly affects decisions, even when outcomes are objectively equivalent

notes.replication_notes: This is one of the most robust findings in behavioral economics. Effect typically replicates with similar magnitude across cultures and populations.
notes.implementation_notes: Ensure participants understand the scenario and that both options have identical expected values (200 lives saved/400 lives lost).
notes.common_issues: Some participants may calculate expected values and choose based on risk preference rather than frame. This reduces but does not eliminate the framing effect.
